epigraph.text: You have set a boundary that they may not pass, so that they might not again cover the earth.
epigraph.quote: The value inside is determined by the values on the boundary—the most remarkable fact in analysis.

content.text:
Cauchy's Integral Formula is perhaps the most important result in complex 
analysis. It says that an analytic function's values everywhere inside a 
curve are determined by its values on the curve.

content.title: Cauchy Integral Formula
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic inside and on a simple closed contour $\gamma$ 
(traversed counterclockwise). For any $z_0$ inside $\gamma$:
\[
 f(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_\gamma \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\,dz.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For small $\epsilon > 0$, let $C_\epsilon$ be the circle $|z - z_0| = \epsilon$ 
(counterclockwise). By the deformation principle:
\[
 \oint_\gamma \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\,dz = \oint_{C_\epsilon} \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\,dz.
\]
On $C_\epsilon$, write $f(z) = f(z_0) + [f(z) - f(z_0)]$:
\[
 \oint_{C_\epsilon} \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\,dz 
 = f(z_0) \oint_{C_\epsilon} \frac{dz}{z - z_0} 
 + \oint_{C_\epsilon} \frac{f(z) - f(z_0)}{z - z_0}\,dz.
\]
The first integral equals $2\pi i$ (computed in Section 4.2). For the 
second, continuity of $f$ gives $|f(z) - f(z_0)| < M$ on $C_\epsilon$ 
for some $M \to 0$ as $\epsilon \to 0$. By ML:
\[
 \left|\oint_{C_\epsilon} \frac{f(z) - f(z_0)}{z - z_0}\,dz\right| 
 \leq \frac{M}{\epsilon} \cdot 2\pi\epsilon = 2\pi M \to 0.
\]
Thus $\oint_\gamma \frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\,dz = 2\pi i f(z_0)$.

content.title: Cauchy's Integral Formula for Derivatives
content.content:
Under the same hypotheses, for all $n \geq 0$:
\[
 f^{(n)}(z_0) = \frac{n!}{2\pi i} \oint_\gamma \frac{f(z)}{(z - z_0)^{n+1}}\,dz.
\]
In particular, analytic functions are infinitely differentiable.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Differentiate the Cauchy Integral Formula under the integral sign:
\[
 f'(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_\gamma \frac{d}{dz_0}\left[\frac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\right]\,dz 
 = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \oint_\gamma \frac{f(z)}{(z - z_0)^2}\,dz.
\]
Justifying differentiation under the integral requires showing uniform 
convergence; the result follows by induction.

content.solution:
The function $f(z) = e^z$ is entire, and $z_0 = 1$ is inside $|z| = 2$. 
By the Cauchy Integral Formula:
\[
 \oint_{|z|=2} \frac{e^z}{z-1}\,dz = 2\pi i \cdot f(1) = 2\pi i e.
\]

Here $f(z) = \sin z$ and we need $f''(0)/2!$. We have 
$f''(z) = -\sin z$, so $f''(0) = 0$.
\[
 \oint_{|z|=1} \frac{\sin z}{z^3}\,dz = \frac{2\pi i}{2!} f''(0) = 0.
\]

marginNotes.text:
For from him and through him and to him 
are all things. To him be glory forever. Amen.

marginNotes.author: Augustin-Louis Cauchy
marginNotes.text:
These formulas are the key to the 
whole theory.
